title: Bailout

jokes.text: These kids are renting my old clothes like they're going out of style. Which they never will.
jokes.setup: Kids renting clothes that are going out of style
jokes.punchline: Which they never will - Tom's delusional confidence about his fashion sense
jokes.explanation: Classic Tom delusion packaged as a clever misdirection. The setup makes you think he's aware his clothes are outdated, then reveals his complete lack of self-awareness.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Which they never will. My fashion sense is timeless. I'm a trendsetter. People see what I'm wearing and they think, 'I need to dress like that guy.'

jokes.text: Oh, 'day-yay-yam,' it is kind of a sausage party in here. For the record, would hit it, would hit, would hit.
jokes.setup: Noting the male-heavy clientele
jokes.punchline: Immediately rating each customer's attractiveness inappropriately
jokes.explanation: Establishes Mona-Lisa's inappropriate workplace behavior immediately. The made-up exclamation adds character flavor.

dialogue.speaker: Mona-Lisa
dialogue.line: Oh wow, look at all these guys. Let me just... *checks out each customer* Okay, that one's a solid 7, this one's a 4, oh that guy's definitely a 9...

jokes.text: ♪ Oh, yeah she's my sister. My twin sister, from the same mister.
jokes.setup: Revealing family connection
jokes.punchline: Singing it and using the ridiculous phrase 'same mister'
jokes.explanation: Peak Jean-Ralphio - the singing delivery and absurd rhyming phrase perfectly capture his character. 'Same mister' is delightfully ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: We're the same mister!

jokes.text: ♪ The wo-o-orst. She is the worst person in the world. Huge skank. Terrible. But thank you. It means a lot.
jokes.setup: Thanking Tom for hiring his sister
jokes.punchline: Immediately and elaborately trashing her while still being grateful
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character writing - Jean-Ralphio's complete honesty about his sister while still being grateful. The singing 'wo-o-orst' became iconic. Perfect contradiction.

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: Yo, Tom, I just want to say thank you so much for hiring my sister Mona-Lisa. That's real generous of you, man. I appreciate it.

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: But real talk, she is the worst. Like, she's dumb as a rock, she smells like a truck stop bathroom, and she once tried to microwave a whole chicken still in the package.

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: But seriously, thank you. You're a good dude.

jokes.text: So if you say no, I will start a fire in the bathroom.
jokes.setup: Tom refusing to let her leave early for a concert
jokes.punchline: Threatening arson as a response to workplace policy
jokes.explanation: Perfectly establishes Mona-Lisa's unhinged nature. The casual threat of arson over a minor inconvenience is absurd and character-defining.

dialogue.speaker: Tom Haverford
dialogue.line: You can't leave early. We have a strict policy.

dialogue.speaker: Mona-Lisa Saperstein
dialogue.line: Oh, so you're not gonna let me go? Fine. I'm gonna burn this place down.

dialogue.speaker: Tom Haverford
dialogue.line: Mona-Lisa, you can't threaten arson.

dialogue.speaker: Mona-Lisa Saperstein
dialogue.line: I'm not threatening it. I'm promising it.

jokes.text: ♪ The wo-o-o-orst. She's the worst in the world.
jokes.setup: Mona-Lisa getting her way
jokes.punchline: Jean-Ralphio's sung commentary on his sister
jokes.explanation: Callback to his earlier song, reinforcing both the catchphrase and his brutal honesty about his sister.

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: She's gonna get her way, she's gonna get her way, she always gets her way. Oh, why do I even try? I know that she's gonna get her way.

jokes.text: I could do, uh, Shoah. Okay, Paths Of Glory it is.
jokes.setup: Offering a more upbeat movie than Paths of Glory
jokes.punchline: Suggesting a 9-hour Holocaust documentary as the 'upbeat' alternative
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy gold - suggesting the Holocaust documentary Shoah as a more upbeat alternative to a WWI execution film. Shows Dennis's complete disconnect from normal entertainment.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Well, if you want something more upbeat than Paths of Glory, how about a 9-hour Holocaust documentary?

jokes.text: Which is why I give E.T. 1 1/2 stars.
jokes.setup: Reviewing E.T. as unbelievable
jokes.punchline: Giving the beloved classic a terrible rating for being unrealistic
jokes.explanation: Perfect Perd Hapley - missing the point entirely of a fantasy film by judging it on realism. The low star rating adds to the absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Perd Hapley
dialogue.line: E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial is a beloved classic, but let's be honest — completely unrealistic. A alien shows up in a California suburb, befriends a ten-year-old boy, and nobody calls the government? Please. I'm giving E.T. one star.

jokes.text: What's everybody-- where are we?
jokes.setup: Andy lying on the floor after the movie
jokes.punchline: Being completely disoriented and confused about his location
jokes.explanation: Classic Andy confusion. Physical comedy of him being on the floor combined with his complete spatial disorientation.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Where am I? What year is it? Did I just see a movie or did I have a stroke?

jokes.text: Why would I do that? All movies are online for free. I'm watching Iron Man 2 on my phone right now.
jokes.setup: Leslie asking him to rent something to help the store
jokes.punchline: Admitting he's illegally streaming while talking to a government official
jokes.explanation: Good situational irony - openly admitting to piracy while a government official is trying to save the business he's undermining.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Would you be interested in renting a movie from us?

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: No thanks, I'm actually illegally streaming everything at home.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Oh... well, I'm a government official, so...

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: Right, I probably shouldn't have said that.

jokes.text: But if you want a film about the ocean, we have a documentary about the brutality of Japan's whaling industry. It's called Tears Of My Blowhole. And it's--it's nauseating.
jokes.setup: Leslie wanting to rent Finding Nemo
jokes.punchline: Offering a depressing documentary with a ridiculous pun title
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Dennis's pretentious art house preferences. 'Tears Of My Blowhole' is an absurd title that sounds real enough to be believable.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I want to rent Finding Nemo.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: How about instead you rent 'Finding Nemo's Remains: A Deep Sea Tragedy'? It's a depressing documentary about fish extinction.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That sounds horrible.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: The tagline is 'He wasn't lost. He was dead the whole time.' We call it 'Finding Nemesis.'

jokes.text: Oh, we only have Japanese slime candy and, um, Bulgarian wheat balls.
jokes.setup: Leslie wanting to buy normal red vines candy
jokes.punchline: Only having bizarre foreign candy options
jokes.explanation: Reinforces Dennis's impractical, art-house-only approach to business. The specific foreign candy names are absurd enough to be funny.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I just want some normal Red Vines. You know, classic American candy.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: Sorry, we're all out. But I've got some great options. How about these Norwegian licorice whips? Or these Japanese pork-flavored gummies?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Pork-flavored? Dennis, these are insane.

dialogue.speaker: Dennis
dialogue.line: There's also this Lithuanian beet candy and some Thai fish-paste lozengers.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: How is this what we have?

jokes.text: All these kids have fathers and mothers who have birthed them and are guiding them through life. Yep, that's how it works.
jokes.setup: Chris making an obvious observation about families
jokes.punchline: Ann's dry confirmation of this basic fact
jokes.explanation: Mild humor from Chris's tendency to over-explain obvious things and Ann's patient response.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Families are really important to people.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Yeah.

jokes.text: I am 100% certain that I am 0% sure of what I'm going to do.
jokes.setup: Ann asking if he's made his decision
jokes.punchline: Contradictory percentages expressing his uncertainty
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay that mathematically expresses confusion. Very Chris Traeger in its precise but contradictory language.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Have you made your decision?

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: I'm 60% sure I want to do it, but I'm also 70% sure I don't want to do it.

jokes.text: Why don't you make Tom your sack of flour, see how it feels to parent him a little?
jokes.setup: Ben suggesting Chris practice parenting
jokes.punchline: Implies Tom is childish enough to serve as practice baby
jokes.explanation: Clever setup that works on multiple levels and drives the episode's B-plot

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: You should practice on Tom.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: What do you mean?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: He's basically a baby anyway.

jokes.text: 100%. She's a total klepto. And nympho and pyro-- all those things.
jokes.setup: Ben suggesting Mona-Lisa is stealing
jokes.punchline: Confirming she's a kleptomaniac, nymphomaniac, and pyromaniac
jokes.explanation: Great escalation - each 'maniac' makes her worse, and Jean-Ralphio's casual delivery makes it funnier. The '-o' suffix pattern is satisfying.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Is Mona-Lisa stealing from us?

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: Yeah, she's a kleptomaniac, a nymphomaniac, and a pyromaniac.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: That's... that's three different things.

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: I know. She's got a lot going on.

jokes.text: Ben, that is literally the greatest idea in human history.
jokes.setup: Ben suggesting Tom as a practice baby
jokes.punchline: Leslie's extreme hyperbolic praise for a simple idea
jokes.explanation: Pure Leslie Knope - her tendency to use extreme hyperbole for mundane things. 'Literally' and 'greatest in human history' are peak Leslie.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: We could use Tom as a practice baby.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ben, that is the most brilliant, innovative, forward-thinking idea anyone has ever had in the entire history of human civilization. You should be awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics. We should name a city after you. Future generations will study your genius in schools. You've basically solved all of humanity's problems with that one sentence.

jokes.text: I heard 'sack of flour' and 'high school.' Are you dingdongs making fake drugs for sophomores? Because if true, this guy wants in.
jokes.setup: Overhearing part of the conversation about practice babies
jokes.punchline: Assuming they're making fake drugs and wanting to join
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection - taking innocent words and jumping to the most illegal conclusion. Perfect Jean-Ralphio criminal mindset and eagerness to participate.

dialogue.speaker: Jean-Ralphio
dialogue.line: Wait, you guys are making fake babies? Dude, I'm in. I thought you said fake drugs.

jokes.text: Good morning, April. What's your secret? I'm thinking about applying to veterinary school in Bloomington. Oh, my God, April, that's great!
jokes.setup: April threatening to kill Ann if she tells her secret
jokes.punchline: The 'secret' being something completely wholesome and positive
jokes.explanation: Great subversion - April's dark threat followed by a completely innocent career aspiration. Shows character growth while maintaining her threatening persona.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: If you tell anyone about this, I will actually kill you.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: About what? That you've been volunteering at the animal shelter on weekends?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Yes. Now you're dead.

jokes.text: - as a janitor... - Nurse.
jokes.setup: April asking Ann to write a recommendation
jokes.punchline: April getting Ann's job wrong despite knowing her well
jokes.explanation: Classic April dismissiveness - deliberately getting Ann's important job wrong. The quick correction shows Ann's practiced patience.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I need you to write me a recommendation letter.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Of course! For what?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: My job application to be a professional horse whisperer.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: April, you work in the Parks Department.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I know. Just tell them about my experience as a veterinary surgeon.

jokes.text: Because you hooked up with him? I don't hook up with everyone I know. Although, yes, we did hook up.
jokes.setup: April assuming Ann knows the vet through hookups
jokes.punchline: Ann denying it then immediately confirming it
jokes.explanation: Good structure - Ann's defensive denial immediately undercut by the truth. Shows April knows Ann better than Ann wants to admit.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: You know the vet through hookups, right?

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: No! What? No.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: ...Okay, yes.

jokes.text: No, you have to be my friend. No, that is so much worse.
jokes.setup: April thinking she has to be Ann's slave
jokes.punchline: Being told friendship is required and finding it worse than slavery
jokes.explanation: Perfect April - the idea of friendship is literally worse than slavery to her. Great character insight delivered economically.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Wait, so you're saying we have to be friends?

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Well, yes. That's what coworkers do.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Ugh. This is worse than slavery.

jokes.text: Because you hooked up with him? I don't hook up with everyone I know. Although, yes, we did hook up.
jokes.setup: April assumes Ann knows the vet through hookup
jokes.punchline: Ann denies the pattern then immediately confirms this specific case
jokes.explanation: Nice self-aware moment about Ann's dating history

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: So you're saying you've slept with the vet?

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: What? No! I don't just sleep with every person I meet.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: But you've slept with this vet.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Yes.

jokes.text: Anything can be a slam poem if you say it like this. It's pointless.
jokes.setup: Leslie defending slam poetry
jokes.punchline: Ron demonstrating that anything said with slam poetry inflection is pointless
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-joke where Ron proves his point by demonstrating it. The self-referential nature and Ron's deadpan delivery make it exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I have something to say. *slams hand on table* We need to cut the budget by 40%. We will lose 200 jobs. The Parks Department will be gutted. *slams hand again* This is a disaster.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: See? Ron, you're doing it!

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Doing what?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Slam poetry! You made it sound powerful!

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I wasn't doing slam poetry. I was just talking about something important. If I said the same thing like this — *speaks monotone* 'we need to cut the budget by 40%, we will lose 200 jobs' — it's the same information. The slam poetry doesn't change the content. It's just... noise.

jokes.text: This man is a failure. He is not up to snuff. His business is failing, and you're bailing it out. This is a bailout, and I don't like it.
jokes.setup: Ron continuing his slam poetry demonstration
jokes.punchline: Delivering harsh criticism of Dennis in slam poetry style
jokes.explanation: Great callback that extends the slam poetry joke while advancing his actual argument. The escalating harshness in slam format is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Dennis, you are a blight upon this earth. Your incompetence knows no bounds. You waddle through life like a penguin with a drinking problem, leaving destruction in your wake.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Your voice is like a thousand dying cats. Your face, a monument to poor life choices. And your haircut—dear God, your haircut is a cry for help that no one will answer.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: You are the human equivalent of a participation trophy. Useless. Unwanted. A constant reminder of society's failures.

jokes.text: I once saw her punch a police horse in the face.
jokes.setup: Explaining why Mona-Lisa scares him
jokes.punchline: Revealing she physically assaulted a police horse
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd and memorable. Perfect economy - one sentence that establishes how unhinged Mona-Lisa is. The specific detail of a police horse makes it iconic.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Mona-Lisa scares me because she physically assaulted a police horse.

jokes.text: You're a smart, successful young man with an adorable, little belly.
jokes.setup: Ben giving Tom a pep talk about standing up for himself
jokes.punchline: Including an awkward compliment about Tom's belly
jokes.explanation: Great awkward Ben moment - trying to be supportive but making it weird with the belly comment. Very in character for his social awkwardness.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Tom, you need to stand up for yourself more. You're a capable guy.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: You've got... you've got a really solid midsection. I mean that.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Thanks, Ben.

jokes.text: And if she murders me, tell Jean-Ralphio to clear my browser history.
jokes.setup: Tom worried about confronting Mona-Lisa
jokes.punchline: His final request reveals embarrassing internet habits
jokes.explanation: Modern humor that captures both Tom's vanity and universal internet anxiety

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Look, I know Mona-Lisa's been spreading rumors about me, but I'm not comfortable confronting her. She's... intimidating.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: If anything happens to me, I just want you to know: please delete my browser history.

jokes.text: That would be like giving food to a mortally wounded animal instead of slitting its throat and properly utilizing its meat and pelt.
jokes.setup: Ron arguing against government bailouts
jokes.punchline: Using an extremely graphic hunting analogy in a city council meeting
jokes.explanation: Peak Ron Swanson - taking a political argument to an extreme place with hunting metaphors. Dark but perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: You want the government to bail out these failing businesses? That's like asking a hunter to shoot a deer in the face because it's having trouble finding food. It's not mercy, it's weakness. And then you'd have venison all over city hall.

jokes.text: Please stop talking about wounded meat.
jokes.setup: Ron's graphic hunting analogy
jokes.punchline: Leslie's disgusted but understated request
jokes.explanation: Good reaction beat. Leslie's polite but firm request to stop the disturbing imagery is perfectly in character for her dealing with Ron.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm going to need you to stop making hunting metaphors about women.

jokes.text: I haven't rented it, actually, yet. But I like the idea that there is a place where I could rent Rashomon.
jokes.setup: Being asked about her favorite part of Rashomon
jokes.punchline: Admitting she's never seen it but likes having the option
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Leslie's sometimes superficial cultural advocacy. She supports the concept more than the actual content.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I've never actually seen Rashomon, but I love that it exists.

jokes.text: You are so freaking sexy right now, I could crap my pants.
jokes.setup: Tom finally standing up to her
jokes.punchline: Mona-Lisa finding his assertiveness arousing in the grossest way possible
jokes.explanation: Perfectly disgusting Mona-Lisa response. The crude expression of arousal undermines Tom's attempt at authority and shows how unpredictable she is.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Mona-Lisa, I'm not going to let you treat me like this anymore. I deserve respect.

dialogue.speaker: Mona-Lisa
dialogue.line: Oh wow. That's actually really hot. I'm getting wet right now.

jokes.text: Yao Ming's torso, Napoleon's brain, and The Hunchback of Notre Dame's hunchback.
jokes.setup: Building the perfect guy after Ann gives normal answers
jokes.punchline: April's deliberately bizarre and contradictory combination
jokes.explanation: Brilliant April subversion of a normal game. The hunchback detail is especially absurd - wanting a deformity as a positive trait. Pure April contrarianism.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I want a guy who's really into cooking, but also a vegetarian, but also loves steak. Someone who's really smart, but also really dumb. A guy who's really tall, but also really short. Someone who's really rich, but also completely broke. A guy who's really handsome, but also really ugly. Someone who's really confident, but also has severe anxiety. A guy who's really outgoing, but also a total introvert.

jokes.text: I'm kind of just fracking for friendship.
jokes.setup: Ann explaining her persistence with April
jokes.punchline: Comparing friendship efforts to controversial drilling technique
jokes.explanation: Clever metaphor connecting friendship pursuit to fracking - both involve drilling deep for something valuable despite environmental concerns (April's hostility).

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: I'm like fracking for friendship. I keep drilling and drilling until I hit something.

jokes.text: I was on food stamps. I was on Welfare. And nobody ever helped me.
jokes.setup: Complaining about government handouts
jokes.punchline: Not realizing food stamps and welfare ARE government help
jokes.explanation: Classic political irony - someone opposing government help while listing all the government help they received. Sharp observation about political hypocrisy.

dialogue.speaker: Ron Swanson
dialogue.line: I don't understand why the government insists on handing out food stamps and welfare to people who won't work. It's a disgrace.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Ron, food stamps and welfare ARE government help.

dialogue.speaker: Ron Swanson
dialogue.line: Yes, well... that's different.

jokes.text: We started making out. Hard. Next thing you know, we're back at my place, having sex. We're together now.
jokes.setup: Ben asking how Tom's confrontation with Mona-Lisa went
jokes.punchline: It immediately turned into sex and a relationship
jokes.explanation: Shows Tom's complete inability to handle conflict - his attempt at authority backfired spectacularly. Typical Tom romantic chaos.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: It immediately turned into sex and a relationship

jokes.text: Hey, bitch, I need to borrow some money to do something that is none of your damn beeswax, namely, I need to wax my bee.
jokes.setup: Mona-Lisa asking Tom for money
jokes.punchline: Crude wordplay connecting 'beeswax' phrase to actual bee waxing
jokes.explanation: Typical crude Mona-Lisa wordplay with sexual undertones

dialogue.speaker: Mona-Lisa
dialogue.line: Tom, I need to borrow some money.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Mona-Lisa, that's none of your beeswax.

dialogue.speaker: Mona-Lisa
dialogue.line: Actually, I'm thinking about getting into the bee waxing business.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Wait, so it IS your beeswax?

jokes.text: Hey, bitch, I need to borrow some money to do something that is none of your damn beeswax, namely, I need to wax my bee.
jokes.setup: Mona-Lisa rudely asking Tom for money
jokes.punchline: Explaining the reason with a ridiculous bee/beeswax pun
jokes.explanation: The pun is crude but clever, and shows Mona-Lisa's complete lack of boundaries in their new relationship. The euphemism adds character depth.

dialogue.speaker: Mona-Lisa
dialogue.line: Yo, Tom, I need to borrow some money.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Why should I lend you money?

dialogue.speaker: Mona-Lisa
dialogue.line: Because it's none of your beeswax.

jokes.text: These braids you did are making my brain hurt.
jokes.setup: Ann asking April to choose something
jokes.punchline: April blaming her inability to think on Ann's hairstyling
jokes.explanation: Typical April - finding something negative about a nice gesture and connecting it to physical discomfort in an absurd way.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Just pick something, April.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I can't think. Your hair is distracting me. It looks like a llama died on your head.

jokes.text: No, anything you can say to Ann, you can say to me because we're best friends this week.
jokes.setup: Chris wanting to talk to Ann privately
jokes.punchline: April sarcastically claiming temporary best friendship
jokes.explanation: Good April line showing her awareness of the artificial nature of their friendship arrangement. The time limit makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Oh, I'll just go. Ann and I are best friends now. Temporarily.

jokes.text: Hey, let's go put on our pj's and make White Russians. So fun.
jokes.setup: Ann forcing friendship activities
jokes.punchline: April sarcastically suggesting stereotypical sleepover activities
jokes.explanation: April's deadpan delivery of cliché friendship activities shows her discomfort while still participating in the arrangement.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: We could braid each other's hair and talk about boys we like.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Or we could paint our nails and stay up until like, 9 PM talking about our feelings.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Or we could just sit in a circle and judge other girls. That's what friends do, right?

jokes.text: You have made us porn peddlers!
jokes.setup: The video store has turned into a porn shop
jokes.punchline: Ron's dramatic declaration about the government's new role
jokes.explanation: Great escalation and perfect Ron reaction. 'Porn peddlers' is a delightfully old-fashioned term that fits his character perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Government has no business telling people what they can and cannot watch in the privacy of their own homes. But apparently, it has every business turning a beloved video rental establishment into a den of pornography.

jokes.text: You have made us porn peddlers!
jokes.setup: Leslie realizes what the video store has become
jokes.punchline: Her horror at the city's accidental involvement in porn
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie outrage at unintended consequences

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Wait, what? The video store is a front for a porn operation?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Apparently.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: And the city has been unknowingly complicit in this? Oh my God, we need to shut this down immediately. This is a disaster. A pornographic disaster.

jokes.text: So I'm thrilled to tell you that I'm playing Leslie Knope in the porn version of the story of this government bailout!
jokes.setup: Brandi thanking Leslie for supporting her work
jokes.punchline: Revealing she's made a porn about this specific situation
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation - the situation has become so meta that there's already a porn parody of it. Leslie's horror makes it even funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Brandi Maxxxx
dialogue.line: Thank you so much for supporting my work, Leslie. It really means a lot.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Of course! I'm always here for you.

dialogue.speaker: Brandi Maxxxx
dialogue.line: In fact, I was so inspired by your support that I made a porn about this exact situation.

jokes.text: This never happened.
jokes.setup: April and Ann having a genuine bonding moment singing together
jokes.punchline: April immediately denying the emotional connection
jokes.explanation: Perfect April - immediately backtracking from any emotional vulnerability. The economy and character consistency make it work well.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: That didn't mean anything. We're not friends. Don't tell anyone about this.

jokes.text: Much less a bran muffin, which is the highest honor that flour can achieve.
jokes.setup: Ben's metaphor about Tom as flour becoming bread
jokes.punchline: Declaring bran muffin as the peak of flour achievement
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurd logic - treating bran muffin as an aspirational life goal. Very Ben to overthink a metaphor to this degree.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Tom is like flour. He starts out as nothing, and then with the right environment and some heat, he becomes bread.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: That's actually really nice, man.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: But then he becomes a bran muffin, which is the peak of flour achievement.

jokes.text: Oh, I'm sorry, guys. I--no one ever lets me talk this long. I just got lost.
jokes.setup: Andy giving surprisingly good parenting advice
jokes.punchline: Getting overwhelmed by being allowed to talk and losing coherence
jokes.explanation: Great meta moment where Andy breaks character awareness. Shows that he actually has good insights but gets overwhelmed by attention.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: You know, being a parent is all about being present and listening to your kids. Really hearing what they have to say, you know? It's about creating a safe space where they feel valued and—wait, am I allowed to keep talking? Because I have so much more. Did you know that consistency is key? And also snacks. Kids need snacks. But like, healthy snacks. Or just snacks. Any snacks really. And you gotta be patient, super patient, because kids are basically tiny drunk people who can't operate doors, and—oh man, I'm on a roll here—you should definitely read to them, but not like boring books, fun books with pictures, or maybe audiobooks while you're driving, which is actually genius when you think about it, and oh! Make sure they know you love them every single day, multiple times a day, maybe tattoo it on your forehead so they see it when they wake up, and also establish boundaries, but like, fun boundaries, and—I'm sorry, what was the question?

jokes.text: I know what I'm about, son.
jokes.setup: Waiter pointing out the party platter serves 12 people
jokes.punchline: Ron's confident assertion that he can handle it
jokes.explanation: Iconic Ron Swanson line. Perfect economy and supreme confidence about his eating abilities. Became a meme-worthy quote.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I can eat twelve people.

jokes.text: These will not be necessary.
jokes.setup: Being given utensils for his massive food order
jokes.punchline: Ron dismissing utensils as unnecessary
jokes.explanation: Great Ron moment showing his primitive eating approach. The formality of his dismissal makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I don't need utensils. I have no use for them.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Food is already in its simplest form. Eating it with instruments is over-complicating things.

jokes.text: A baby-naming book. I hope it works out with Chris. And if it doesn't, I hope it works out with someone.
jokes.setup: April giving Ann a gift
jokes.punchline: Showing genuine care while maintaining her detached delivery
jokes.explanation: Nice character moment showing April's hidden care for Ann. The practical gift and awkward well-wishes are perfectly April.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I got you something. It's practical and useful, which is why I'm giving it to you instead of keeping it for myself. I don't do gifts well, but you're my best friend, so I'm making an exception. Don't make this weird by getting emotional about it.

jokes.text: Well, I guess your uter-you and my uter-me are now our uter-us.
jokes.setup: Chris agreeing to be Ann's sperm donor
jokes.punchline: Making terrible uterus puns about their collaboration
jokes.explanation: Very Chris Traeger - turning an emotional moment into an awkward pun. The terrible wordplay is perfectly in character but not especially clever.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: I'm happy to help you and Ben with this. It's a beautiful thing you're doing.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Thank you so much, Chris. We really appreciate it.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: You know what they say - this is really going to be a womb with a view.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Oh my god.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: I mean, we're going to have such a strong cervix- I mean, service. Partnership. We're really going to... uterus well together.

jokes.text: I'm glad you're here, Dong Swanson. The local video store's about to go under. We have to save it. Oh, this is the wrong movie.
jokes.setup: Screening what should be The Sound of Music
jokes.punchline: It's actually the porn parody of the episode's events, with terrible character names
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta ending - the porn version of the episode is shown within the episode. 'Dong Swanson' is an absurd but perfect porn name.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What is this? This isn't The Sound of Music!

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: It appears to be a pornographic parody.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh my God, look at these character names. 'Porn Leslie'? 'Porn Ron'? This is ridiculous!

tmdb_overview: Leslie is determined to help a local video store stay in business, while Chris offers Tom advice on how to deal with a difficult employee. Elsewhere, Ann resorts to blackmail to get April to be her friend.
insight: Budget crisis forces Leslie into absurd fundraising schemes; character comedy carries 78-point episode.
